title: Workflows & demostrations
text:
Workflows & demonstrations

The user interface
This image displays the Navigation Guide for ADMETShiny (Version 1.0.0), an open-source R Shiny web application designed for the management, calculation, filtering, and selection of drug-likeness potential molecules in early-stage drug discovery. The user interface features a top horizontal navigation bar split into nine numbered modules indicated by green hex icons: (1) Home, which returns to the main landing page; (2) SwissADME Manager, for calculating and analyzing molecular properties; (3) CDK & webchem, providing access to cheminformatics tools for structure processing and chemical analysis.





(4) ADMETlab 3.0 Manager and (5) Deep-PK Manager, which run predictive ADMET and pharmacokinetic models using third-party and deep learning architectures; (6) Report, for customizing and exporting analysis results; (7) About, detailing project and author information; (8) First Steps, offering step-by-step tutorials for new users; and (9) Documentation, which hosts technical guides and vignettes. Below this primary interface menu, the dashboard is split into two bottom sections highlighted by green pointers: a Description box on the left, summarizing the app’s integration of bioinformatics and cheminformatics workflows for molecular descriptor visualization, and a How to cite? box on the right, providing the formal 2026 academic citation and the GitHub repository link



The datasets example
This image details the user interface of the SwissADME Manager module within ADMETShiny, showcasing the data uploading, filtering, and table visualization capabilities. On the left side, the dashboard features a control panel where users can upload their chemical data via the Upload SwissADME Dataset tool (3), which currently shows a completed upload of an example CSV file (dataset_swissadme_example.csv). Below this, the Drug-likeness Filters section (4) allows users to apply customizable filters based on popular medicinal chemistry criteria, including Lipinski, Veber, Ghose, Egan, and Muegge rules, along with adjustable threshold inputs for maximum molecular weight, logP, hydrogen bond acceptors/donors, and maximum Lipinski violations.





The main central area displays the Filtered Results table under the Filtered Molecules tab (6), which renders a structured overview of the compounds that meet the set criteria. This interactive data grid displays key molecular descriptors such as the Molecule ID, Canonical SMILES, chemical Formula, Molecular Weight (MW), number of Heavy Atoms, and number of Aromatic Heavy Atoms. Users can utilize the Search bar (8) located at the top right of the table to quickly filter molecules by name, SMILES string, or specific properties. Additionally, the interface includes top tabs for Data Preview (5) and Plots (7) to seamlessly toggle between the raw dataset, the refined subsets, and data distributions, providing an intuitive, integrated workflow for compound prioritization.


You probably need a report
The executive summary and statistical breakdown confirm that 100% of the library was successfully retained, exhibiting average baseline properties such as a Molecular Weight of 392.44, a LogP of 3.33, and a TPSA of 90.53. The library demonstrates high quality with a mean composite drug-likeness score of 85.4 out of 100, where 80.5% of the compounds achieve an “Excellent” classification.





These metrics are supported by three robust visualizations: a BOILED-Egg plot mapping gastrointestinal absorption and blood-brain barrier permeability alongside P-glycoprotein efflux status, a stacked bar chart detailing individual rule violations across standard medicinal chemistry criteria (Lipinski, Ghose, Veber, Egan, and Muegge), and a score distribution histogram that visually confirms the sharp clustering of the vast majority of the compounds within the highest drug-likeness tier.


The conclusions







 Back to top

title: Documentation
text:
Theory & Documentation

Initial considerations
This ADMETShiny package contains four sections, each with its own independent workflow. The user will ultimately be able to generate a comprehensive report that includes the main characteristics of the applied filters (Executive Summary per model, Statistics of Key properties, Drug-likeness Filter Results, BOILED-Egg ADMET Classification, Additional Drug-likeness Metrics, Composite Drug-likeness Score & graphs: BOILED-Egg, Distributions to MW, TPSA, LogP histograms, correlations between physicochemical properties & cluster heatmaps per model).



Introduction and Architectural Overview
ADMETShiny is an R package providing an interactive Shiny application and a programmatic API for the management, calculation, filtering, visualization, and exploratory analysis of molecular descriptors and ADMET properties. It integrates four data sources with fundamentally different algorithms and data schema, standardizing them into a unified pipeline.
The Design Philosophy of this package include an architected around three core principles. For one hand a single generic LogP column is used across all modules, with thresholds from original publications (That’s include SwissADME values). This ensures that filters work identically regardless of data provenance.

The UI is composed of independent tab modules (SwissADME, CDK, ADMETLab, Deep-PK and report), each with its own UI builder, reactive values, and server logic, sharing a common set of computational functions.


Package Structure








Layer
Files
Purpose




Core API
filters.R, data_fixers.R, cdk_webchem.R, plots.R, plots_cluster.R, plot_customization.R, report.R
Exported functions for filtering normalization, visualization, reporting


Shiny App
app_ui.R, app_server.R, ui.R, dark_mode.R, run_app.R
Interactive web application


Infrastructure
admetshiny-package.R, globals.R, utils-pipe.R, boiled_egg_data.R
Package metadata, constants, imports, data



This separation ensures that the computational functions can be used independently of the Shiny application, either in R scripts or embedded in other packages.


Theoretical background

ADME
ADME is the four-letter acronym for absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion. That has described pharmacokinectics for 50 years (Doogue and Polasek 2013). These terms were first presented together in English by Nelson in 1961, rephrasing resorption, distribution, consumption and elimination used by Teorell in 1937 (Nelson 1961). ADME as originally used for the interpretation of behavior molecules: entering the body (A), moving about the body (D), changing within the body (M) and leaving the body (E). Currently the definition is important for the characterization of potential drugs.
In the last years, a large variety of in silico methods share the objective of predicting ADME parameters form molecular structure. For this reason, the cheminformaticians and bioinformaticians developed different molecular descriptors mined from chemical structures (Daina et al. 2017).


Drug-likeness
Drug development is difficult, not only because of complex process and long development cycles, but also because of the high rate of candidates at all stages (Wei et al. 2020). That’s reason, the comprehensive data on the loss of candidates from the four largest pharmaceutical companies, showed that the control of physicochemical properties during compound optimization is beneficial in improving the quality of candidates (Waring et al. 2015). The descriptors of drug-likeness have gradually been perfected over time.


TPSA
Topological polar surface area (TPSA), which makes use of functional group contributions based on a large database of structures (Chuang et al. 2020). This molecular descriptor (MD) calculated as the sum of surface contributions from polar atoms (Prasanna and Doerksen 2009). TPSA is derived directly from the molecular topology, that the case correspond to SMILES (Beck et al. 2022). Currently this MD is expressed in square angstroms \((Å²)\) and are highly predictive of how a molecule behaves in the body.


Lipophilicity
Since experimental LogP measurements are unavailable for most virtual compounds, computational chemistry relies on predictive algorithms to estimate lipophilicity. Several approaches have been proposed, including atom-fragment methods, fragment-correction schemes (Morak-Młodawska et al. 2023). Consequently, differents software platforms report different numerical estimations of LogP even when evaluating the same molecular structure. For example differents Web-servers for ADMET properties request several algorithms: Wildman-Crippen atom-fragment model (WLOGP), Atom-fragment model with correction factors (XLOGP), Ghose-Crippen atoms fragments (ALOGP) (Abramson et al. 2024).
The quantification lipohilicity are described with numerical estimators of the same physicochemical properties rather than interchangeable values.
\[\mathrm{LogP}_{\mathrm{WLOGP}}
\not\equiv
\mathrm{LogP}_{\mathrm{XLOGP}}
\not\equiv
\mathrm{LogP}_{\mathrm{ALOGP}}
\not\equiv
\mathrm{LogP}_{\mathrm{ML}}\]
For this package, it’s possible define a different representation for this equation. This equation could described with the next form:
\[\mathrm{WLOGP}
\not\equiv
\mathrm{XLOGP}
\not\equiv
\mathrm{ML\text{-}LogP}
\not\equiv
\mathrm{DL\text{-}LogP}\]
Differents model in ADMETShiny use a consensus liphophilicity. This package use a LogP as logical representation to the liphophilicity.



Drug-likeness filters: Mathematical foundations
ADMETShiny implements five classical drug-likeness filters. Each filter is implemented as two-layer system:

Individual physicochemical property thresholds
A pre-computed violations count. This approach provides redundant safety: a compound must pass both the explicit thresholds and have a violations count within tolerance.


Lipinski Rule of Five
V_Lipinski = I(MW > 500) + I(LogP > 5) + I(HBA > 10) + I(HBD > 5)  where I(condition) = 1 if condition is true, 0 otherwise  Filter: compound passes if V_Lipinski <= max_violations (default: 0)
The original Lipinski paper use MLOGP <=4.15(Lipinski et al. 1997). ADMETShiny uses the generic LogP <=5 . This is bacause MLOGP is a specific algorithm (Moriguchi topological method) that is only available in SwissADME and R alternative versions with reticulate. Using LogP <=5 (The threshold stated in the original Rule-of-Five as LogP < 5) makes the filter source-agnostic. The violations columns is recalculated by computeViolationColumns() consoles to use this threshold, overwriting SwissADME pre-computed values.


Veber oral bioavailability Rule
V_Veber = I(RB > 10 OR (TPSA > 140 AND (HBA + HBD) > 12))  Note: This is a BINARY violation (0 or 1), not a sum. The two polarity conditions (TPSA and HBA+HBD) are ALTERNATIVES (OR), not cumulative (AND).  Filter: compound passes if V_Veber == 0
The original paper state that good oral bioavailability is associated with RB<=10 & (TPSA<=140 or HBA+HBD<=12)(Veber et al. 2002). Many implementations incorrectly use and between the polarity conditions. ADMETShiny correctly use OR, matching the original paper. The implementation creates a temporary column .hb_sum = HBADconsolas inside the filter pipeline, then removes it with select(.hb_sum)consolas to avoid polluting the output .


Ghose Qualifying Range
V_Ghose = I(MW < 160 OR MW > 480)       + I(MR < 40 OR MR > 130)       + I(LogP < -0.4 OR LogP > 5.6)       + I(#Heavy atoms < 20 OR #Heavy atoms > 70)  Filter: compound passes if V_Ghose <= max_violations (default: 0)
The Ghose filter requires MR (Molar Refractivity) and #Heavy atoms . SwissADME provides both natively. ADMETLab 3.0 and Deep-pK do not, so fixADMETLab() and fixDeepPK() calculate them from SMILES using CDK before the filter can be applied. At the case of Deep-pK the calculate is execute in another option because the pK variables are independents, for this package that’s not optimize and limit the automatic workflow.


Egan PSA-LogP rule
V_Egan = I(TPSA > 131.6) + I(LogP > 5.88)  Filter: compound passes if V_Egan <= max_violations (default: 0)
Two properties using this formula. The thresholds are derived from multivariate statistical analysis of oral bioavailability data (Egan et al. 2000).


Muegge Pharmacophore filter
V_Muegge = I(MW < 200 OR MW > 600)         + I(LogP < -2 OR LogP > 5)         + I(HBA > 10)         + I(HBD > 5)         + I(RB > 15)         + I(TPSA > 150)  Filter: compound passes if V_Muegge <= max_violations (default: 0)
The original Muegge rule include a pharmacophore points >=4 criteria (HBA + HBD +rings + cations + anions>=4). This is not available from any of the four data sources. For example, HBA y HBD is available in the four datasets that the user could report. Cations and anion are not available, that’s rule not considered. In the other hand, ADMETLab 3.0 dataset have nRing, but in SwissADME, Deep-pK and rCDK this category not. In the package that is a limitation.
# Una molécula pasa si:
#Rotatable bonds <= 15
AND  MW >= 200 AND MW <= 600
AND  LogP >= -2 AND LogP <= 5
AND  TPSA <= 150
AND  #H-bond acceptors <= 10
AND  #H-bond donors <= 5
AND  Muegge #violations <= 0  (configurable)


ApplyFilters() Dispatcher
The dispatcher applies selected filters in a fixed order (Lipinski > Veber > Ghose > Egan > Muegge). Each filter is applied to the output of the previous one, so the result is the intersection of all selected rules. The fixed order ensures deterministic behavior.
applyFilters <- function(data, filters, lipinski = list(), ...) {   resultado <- data   if ("Lipinski" %in% filters)     resultado <- do.call(lipinskiFilter, c(list(data = resultado), lipinski))   if ("Veber" %in% filters)     resultado <- do.call(veberFilter, c(list(data = resultado), veber))   # ... (same for Ghose, Egan, Muegge)   resultado }
The do.call() + named parameter list pattern allows the UI to pass a sparse parameter set per filter. Empty lists fall back to each filter’s defaults.



Descriptor harmonization
The package use four prediction engines. The principal problem with them started with the several heterogeneous outputs doubt the differ in descriptor nomenclature, numerical estimators, and property availability. The first solution was resolve with tidyverse package. However was necessary ensure a unified downstream workflow and avoid possible errors in the input datasets and input calculate with rCDK. For this, this package resolved this with a harmonization process. This is performed before any visualization, drug-likeness assessment or BOILED-Egg classification, allowing all analytical modules to operate independently of the original prediction engine.
\[\mathrm{LogP}_{\mathrm{backend}}
\longrightarrow
\mathrm{LogP}_{\mathrm{ADMETshiny}}\]
For this, the internal descriptor schema is defined as:
\[\mathcal{D}
=
\{
MW,\,
LogP,\,
TPSA,\,
HBA,\,
HBD,\,
RB,\,
MR,\,
HeavyAtoms,\,
AromaticHeavyAtoms,\,
GI,\,
BBB,\,
Pgp
\}\]
where every backend is transformed into this common representation.
In the source code for the transformation in the building BOILED-Egg graph is represent as
if ("Consensus Log P" %in% names(data)) {
  data$LogP <- data[["Consensus Log P"]]
} else if ("WLOGP" %in% names(data)) {
  data$LogP <- data$WLOGP
} else if ("MLOGP" %in% names(data)) {
  data$LogP <- data$MLOGP
}
A little example to this show in the block code above. Another example listed for ADMETLab 3.0 datasets as:
  ## 1. Remove the molstr (SVG) column if it exists
  if ("molstr" %in% names(data)) {
    data$molstr <- NULL
  }

  ## 2. Remove the first index column if it exists
  first_col_name <- names(data)[1]
  if (first_col_name == "0" || first_col_name == "") {
    data <- data[, -1, drop = FALSE]
  }

  ## 3. Rename columns from ADMETlab names to the application's standard names
  rename_map <- c(
    "smiles" = "CanonicalSMILES",
    "MW"     = "MW",
    "TPSA"   = "TPSA",
    "nHA"    = "#H-bond acceptors",
    "nHD"    = "#H-bond donors",
    "nRot"   = "#Rotatable bonds",
    "logP"   = "LogP"
  )
This package in fixADMETlab() are not only renaming columns, this too compute missing descriptors for the analysis
  ## 5. Calculate missing properties (MR, #Heavy atoms, #Aromatic heavy atoms)
  ##    from SMILES using CDK. ADMETlab 3.0 does not provide these natively,
  ##    but they are required by the Ghose and Muegge filters.
  missing_props <- setdiff(
    c("MR", "#Heavy atoms", "#Aromatic heavy atoms"),
    names(data)
  )
For this we used rCDK function:
calcCDKDescriptors()
The workflows is define with the next steps:

This harmonization enables identical downstream workflows for physicochemical descriptors analysis, drug-likeness filters, statistical summaries, BOILED-Egg visualization and report generation. The objective is not to force numerical equivalence among different prediction models but to provide a consistent computational interface across heterogeneous software platforms.


Data Standardization Schema
Each data source provides physicochemical descriptors and ADMET predictions computed with different algorithms and named with different conventions. ADMETShiny maps all of them to a unified internal schema.










Standar column
SwissADME
ADMETLab 3.0
Deep-pK
CDK & Webchem




MW
MW
MW
WeightDescriptor (CDK)
WeightDescriptor


LogP
WLOGP
logP
[Log(P)] Predictions or AlogP (CDK)
AlogP (Ghose-Crippen)


TPSA
TPSA
TPSA
TPSADescriptor (CDK)
TPSADescriptor (CDK)


#H Bond acceptors
#H bond acceptors
nHA
nHBAcc (CDK)
nHBAcc


#H Bond donors
#H bond donors
nHD
nHBDon (CDK)
nHBDon


#Rotatable bonds
#Rotatable bonds
nRot
nRotB (CDK)
nRotB


#Heavy atoms
#Heavy atoms
nAtom
nAtom (CDK)
nAtom


MR
MR
AMR
AMR (CDK)
AMR


GI absorption
GI absorption
hia>=0.5 (binarized)
Interpretation text (parsed)
Boiled-Egg polygon test


BBB permeant
BBB permeant
BBB >=0.5 (binarized)
Intepretation text (parsed)
Boiled-Egg polygon test


Pgp substrate
Pgp substrate
pgp_sub >=0.5 (binarized)
Interpretation text (parsed)
Heuristic





The BOILED-Egg model

Mathematical implementation of the BOILED-Egg model
The original BOILED-Egg model operates in the descriptor space:
\[\mathcal{D}
=
\{
WLogP,\,
TPSA\}\] Since WLOGP is unavailable in several supported backends, for this reason ADMETShiny introduces the practical mathematical approximation. THe approximation is list followed:
\[\{WLOGP,\;TPSA\}
\;\approx\;
\{LogP,\;TPSA\}\]
where the generic LogP corresponds to the lipophilicity estimator returned by the selected computational engine. Accordingly, the only approximation introduced in the ADMETshiny implementation concerns the lipophilicity descriptor itself. The geometric decision regions, polygon coordinates, and point-in-polygon classification algorithm remain identical to those defined in the original BOILED-Egg model.


Physical-chemical space in the BOILED-Egg
The BOILED-Egg model (Daina and Zoete 2016) is a visual and computational model that predicts gastrointestinal absorption (GI) and blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability from two physicochemical properties, TPSA (x-axis) and WLOGP (y-axis). The model defines two regions:

White region (HIA): high probability of gastrointestinal absorption
Yellow yolk (BBB): high probability of crossing the blood-brain barrier




Validation of the approximation
ADMETShiny uses the official polygon coordinates published as data S3 in the supplementary material of the original articles. Each polygon is defined by 101 (x,y) points where x = TPSA and y = WLOGP.

The BBB polygon in the below table contains small negative TPSA value (-2.96). This is intentional in the official polygon and reflects the model’s calibration; it does not represent a physical impossibility but rather the mathematical extension of the fitted boundary. That the case the coordinates are stored as internal R object in R/boiled_egg_data.R:
.hia_polygon <- matrix(c(97.8055224368, -2.2270390475, ...), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) .bbb_polygon <- matrix(c(40.9701792513, 0.4062562900, ...), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) # Column 1 = TPSA (x-axis), Column 2 = LogP (y-axis)

Polygon points defined by 101 points


Region
TPSA Range
WLOGP Range
Points




HIA (white)
0.02 - 142.03
-2.25 - 6.84
101


BBB (yellow)
-2.96 - 79.10
0.41 - 5.96
101



The BOILED-Egg is defined with 101 points for WLOGP (Daina and Zoete 2016). These coordinates are stored as internal R objects .hia_polygon, .bbb_polygon in the file R/boiled_egg_data.R and are loaded once when the package is attached.

Point in polygon classification for each molecule with coordinates (TPSA, LogP), the classification is performed using a point in polygon algorithm. ADMETShiny implements two approaches:

sp::point.in.polygon() from the suggested package "sp". This is a C-optimized implementation of the ray-casting algorithm.
A pure-R implementation of the ray-casting algorithm. A ray is cast horizontally from the query point; if it crosses the polygon boundary an odd number of times, the point is inside.

The classification logic correspond to:
if point_in_polygon(TPSA, LogP, HIA_polygon): GI_absorption = 'High' else: GI_absorption = 'Low'  if point_in_polygon(TPSA, LogP, BBB_polygon): BBB_permeant = 'Yes' else: BBB_permeant = 'No'
This package use a BOILED-Egg approximation LogP source was that calibrated with WLOGP (Wildman-Crippen). The accuracy of the classification depends on the LogP algorithm used:









Module
LogP used
Fidelity
Recommendation




SwissADME
WLOGP
Optimal
Use for validation


ADMETLab 3.0
logP (ML model)
Approximate
Exploratory only


Deep-PK
LogP (DL model)
Approximate
Exploratory only


CDK & Webchem
ALogP (Ghose-Crippen)
Approximate
Exploratory only



In this SwissADME module, users can select which LogP variant to use for the BOILED-Egg plot (WLOGP, Consensus LogP, MLOGP, XLOGP3, iLOGP or generic LogP). For other modules, the platform’s own LogP is used, which may introduce classification errors for compounds near the polygon boundaries.









Source
Algorithm
Method Type
Typical difference vs WLOGP




SwissADME WLOGP
Wildman-Crippen
Atom-based fragmentation
Without difference


SwissADME Consensus LogP
Mean of iLOGP, XLOGP3, WLOGP, MLOGP
Ensemble
0.3 - 0.8 units


SwissADME MLOGP
Moriguchi
Topological indices
0.5 -1.5 units


ADMETLab LogP
Proprietary ML model
Machine Learning
0.5 - 1-5 units


Deep-pK LogP
Proprietary DL model
Deep learning
0.5 - 2.0 units


CDK AlogP
Ghose-Crippen
Atom-based fragmentation
0.5 - 1.5 units



For SwissADME Manager, WLOGP is used as the default LogP (matching the BOILED-Egg calibration). For other sources, the platform’s own LogP is used as an approximation. In the SwissADME module’s BOILED-Egg plot, the user can select which LogP variant to use via a dropdown selecto (WLOGP, Consensus LogP, MLOGP, XLOGP3, iLOGP or generic Logp). Generic LogP is an equivalence to WLOGP with SwissADME dataset.

Mathematical implication
If a molecule has WLOGP = 2.0 and ALogP = 1.3 , and the HIA polygon boundary at that TPSA is at LogP = 1.5, the molecule would be classified as high (inside) with WLOGP but low (outside) with AlogP. This boundary effect is the primary source of classification disagreement between modules.


Axis convention
The plot in ADMETShiny package follow the SwissADME convention: X-axis = TPSA (0 to 200), Y-axis = LogP (-2 to 7). The polygon coordinates are stored in this (TPSA, LogP) = (x,y) format, so no transposition is needed when passing to point_in_polygon or geom_polygon.


P-glycoprotein substrate prediction
The Boiled-Egg uses P-gp substrate status only for point colouring (blue=PGP+, red=PGP-), not for GI/BBB classification. Each module obtais P-gp differently:

At the case of CDK, the heuristic equation correspond to the follow:








Modue
Method
Algorithm
Accuracy




SwissADME
CSV
SVM
High (Validated)


ADMETLab 3.0
pgp__sub>0.5
ML model probability threshold
High


Deep-pK
Text parsing (Substrate/Non-substrate)
DL model interpretation
High


CDK & Webchem
Heuristic formula
Literature-based (Seelig 1998a)
Medium-Low (approximation)



Pgp = Yes if:     (MW > 400 AND TPSA > 40 AND (HBA + HBD) >= 8 AND LogP > 1)     OR     (MW > 500 AND LogP > 4 AND TPSA > 40) else: Pgp = No
This heuristic is more selective than the original Seeling criteria (Which only required MW>400) to avoid classifying nearly all drug-like compounds as P-gp substrates (Seelig 1998b). The additional LogP>1 condition was added because the original criteria produced 80% false positive rate on typical drug-like datasets.


Data sources and computational backends

CDK & WebChem
The CDK (Chemistry Development Kit) descriptor calculation follows a three-stage pipeline:
1. SMILES parsing: rcdk::parse.smiles(smiles) -> IAtomContainer objects 2. Molecule configuration:    - convert.implicit.to.explicit(m)  # add explicit hydrogens    - do.aromaticity(m)                 # perceive aromatic systems    - set.atom.types(m)                 # set atom types (modern API) 3. Descriptor evaluation: rcdk::eval.desc(mols, classes) -> data.frame
The AlogPdescriptor is special because it returns three columns:



CDK Column
Description
Mapped to




AlogP
Ghose-Crippen LogP
LogP (generic)


AlogP2
Second LogP model
Dropped


AMR
Approximate Molar Refractivity
MR







Statistical analysis

PCA (Principal Component Analysis)
Pca is computed using stats::prcomp with centering and optional scaling. The implementation handles several edge cases:

Zero-variance columns are removed before prcomp prevent scale division by zero.
If <3 complete observations remain, the function stops with an informative error.
95% confidence ellipses are only draw for groups with >=3 points stat_ellipse() requirement.
Loading arrows are scaled to fit the plot; the scaling factor handles the edge case where all loadings are identical for a PC.
prcompis wrapped in tryCatch if scaling fails, it retries without scaling.

This PCA function is retrieved with the follow mathematical approximation:
X_scaled = scale(X, center = TRUE, scale = scale_data) # If scale_data, each column is divided by its standard deviation  pca = prcomp(X_scaled, center = TRUE, scale. = scale_data) # SVD decomposition: X = U * S * V^T # Scores: pca$x = U * S (n x k matrix) # Loadings: pca$rotation = V (k x k matrix) # Variance explained: (pca$sdev)^2 / sum((pca$sdev)^2)



T-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (T-SNE)
TSNE are good representations for chemical spaces and another categories. This maps are high dimensional images into the two axis for visualization (Zhu et al. 2022). T-SNE is computed using the rtsne package with automatic perplexity adjustment:
max_perplexity = floor((n - 1) / 3)  # Rtsne requirement perplexity = min(user_perplexity, max_perplexity) # Zero-variance columns removed, NaN/Inf replaced with 0 after scaling



Cluster heatmap with dendrogram
Hierarchical clustering is performed using stats::hclust with user-selected linkage method. The heatmap is rendered using base R heatmap(), with couples the dendrogram directly to the heatmap cells.
row_dist = dist(mat, method = 'euclidean') row_hclust = hclust(row_dist, method = method)  # default: ward.D2 col_dist = dist(t(mat), method = 'euclidean') col_hclust = hclust(col_dist, method = method)  heatmap(mat, Rowv = as.dendrogram(row_hclust),              Colv = as.dendrogram(col_hclust), ...)
Base R option is used instead of pheatmap or ComplexHeatmap to minimize dependencies. The function pre-computes both dendograms so the user can pick any linkage, method, then passes them as guaranteeing alignment.


Radar Plot
The radar plot compares up to 5 molecules on 6 physicochemical properties. Properties are normalized 0-1 against the full dataset range (not just the selected subset) to ensure comparability across selections:
 norm(p) = (value - min_full) / (max_full - min_full) # If max_full == min_full, norm = 0.5 (avoids division by zero)
The normalized values are passed to fmsb::radarchart, which requires two header rows (max=1, min=0) followed by the data rows.


Composite Drug-Likeness Score
The composite score (0-100) is the proportion of the five drug-likeness rules that each compound satisfies:
passes[i, rule] = 1 if (violations[rule] == 0), else 0 n_rules = rowSums(!is.na(passes)) score = rowSums(passes, na.rm = TRUE) / max(n_rules, 1) * 100 score[n_rules == 0] = NA  # no data available
Score categories:

Excellent: score>=80 (passes at least 4 of 5 rules)
Aceptable: score 60-79 (passes 3 of 5 rules)
Poor: score<60 (passes fewer than 3 rules)



Case study
The goal is for the user to find a quick design scheme for analyzing a molecule bank. This example will be performed with 200 molecules, attempting to respect the limit allowed by SwissADME. For more information on the user interface and workflow, please refer to the tutorials

title: admetshiny 1.0.0
text:
admetshiny 1.0.0

Initial CRAN release.
Interactive Shiny application with four data-source modules: SwissADME, CDK & webchem, ADMETlab 3.0 and Deep-PK.
Drug-likeness filters: Lipinski, Veber, Ghose, Egan, Muegge.
BOILED-Egg model, PCA, t-SNE, parallel coordinates, violin, radar and correlation heatmap visualizations.
Tanimoto / AGNES structural-similarity clustering.
Dark mode, custom colour palettes and high-resolution figure export.
Codename: Espeletia hartwegiana.





 Back to top

title: admetshiny
text: Provides an interactive shiny application and a toolbox of R functions for the management, calculation, filtering, visualization and exploratory analysis of molecular descriptors and ADMET (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion and Toxicity) properties of small molecules. It integrates workflows from SwissADME, ADMETlab 3.0 and Deep-PK, computes descriptors locally via the Chemistry Development Kit (CDK), and offers drug-likeness filters (Lipinski, Veber, Ghose, Egan, Muegge), the BOILED-Egg model, PCA, t-SNE, radar plots and Tanimoto/AGNES clustering to support compound prioritization in early-stage drug discovery.

title: admetshiny
text: Provides an interactive shiny application and a toolbox of R functions for the management, calculation, filtering, visualization and exploratory analysis of molecular descriptors and ADMET (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion and Toxicity) properties of small molecules. It integrates workflows from SwissADME, ADMETlab 3.0 and Deep-PK, computes descriptors locally via the Chemistry Development Kit (CDK), and offers drug-likeness filters (Lipinski, Veber, Ghose, Egan, Muegge), the BOILED-Egg model, PCA, t-SNE, radar plots and Tanimoto/AGNES clustering to support compound prioritization in early-stage drug discovery.

title: admetshiny
text:
Espeletia hartwegiana
The frailejón is a caulescent rosette reaching up to 4 m in height, with slender stems, inflorescences approximately equal in length to the leaves, and yellow capitula.
It is one of the emblematic plants of the Colombian páramo ecosystem, playing a key ecological role in water regulation, storage, and generation.





Beyond its ecological function, E. hartwegiana supports a unique assemblage of microorganisms, insects, birds, and other high-Andean organisms that depend on páramo habitats. The species also represents an important reservoir of specialized secondary metabolites with promising pharmacological and biotechnological potential.
However, climate change, agricultural expansion, livestock grazing, recurrent fires, and habitat fragmentation are threatening natural populations throughout the northern Andes. Rising temperatures are expected to reduce suitable páramo habitats, forcing populations to migrate to higher elevations where available habitat becomes increasingly limited.





The conservation of Espeletia hartwegiana is therefore essential not only for preserving one of the world’s most biodiverse mountain ecosystems, but also for safeguarding water security, ecological resilience, and the chemical diversity that may support future discoveries in natural product research and drug development.

title: About ADMETShiny
text:
With ADMETShiny, say goodbye to manual ADMET analysis although different Web-Servers. Now, you could management your physicochemical profiles in once only spot.

Finally, you could generate an once report about the principal characteristics of the each model.
If you’re not estimate that’s profile in external websites…, Don’t Worry!, This Shiny include a web-local-server built with rCDK R package and Java SE Development Kit (JDK).

title: About ADMETShiny
section: One for open source and open source for one
text:
One for open source and open source for one
Input data although of different methods (datasets in “.csv”, search in PubChem with CID, common name, inchKey & CAS).

Now, you can calculate drug-likeness properties with CDK & WebChem
The dataset & management filters could apply above to the properties (molecular descriptors) and get very easy reports.







Yes, you could use the console for develop a simple reproducible script
And this isn’t all! You could generate your reports with the console… :)

title: About ADMETShiny
section: The Shiny App for coders & no coders
text:
The Shiny App for coders & no coders
Built for both coders and non-coders, ADMETShiny offers an intuitive graphical interface that makes advanced cheminformatics analyses accessible without programming experience, while remaining fully integrated with the R ecosystem for users who require reproducible, customizable, and extensible workflows.

title: About ADMETShiny
text:
Knowledge structure analysis
ADMETShiny provides a comprehensive collection of interactive modules designed to streamline the early stages of drug discovery by integrating molecular retrieval, descriptor calculation, ADMET prediction, chemical space exploration, structural similarity analysis, and automated reporting into a single user-friendly environment.





Overall, ADMETShiny combines data retrieval, descriptor calculation, predictive modeling, interactive visualization, molecular prioritization, and automated reporting into a unified open-source platform specifically designed to accelerate computational drug discovery and medicinal chemistry research.

title: About the author
text:
About the author
Xavier Clemente García Cevallos is a biologist and researcher student whose work focuses on computational drug discovery, cheminformatics, bioinformatics, natural products, and neurobiology. His research integrates experimental phytochemistry with in silico approaches, including molecular docking, ADMET profiling, molecular dynamics, and software development for reproducible computational workflows. He is the developer of ADMETShiny, an open-source R package designed to facilitate the analysis and visualization of molecular descriptors and ADMET properties.



 Back to top

title: admetshiny
text:
How to install?
Two different forms for install this package..
The next steps:
Steps

Install R and RStudio.

You could choice your R & Rstudio version f your operative system.
I recommended install RStudio for the easyful user interface

Install the pak our remotes packages.
To enjoy all the features of ADMETShiny, we suggest you install all the dependencies listed below.

#All dependencies:
install.packages(c("shiny", "DT", "ggplot2", "dplyr", "magrittr", "rcdk", "webchem", "fmsb", "Rtsne", "ggrepel", "fingerprint", "cluster", "GGally", "openxlsx", "knitr", "rmarkdown", "testthat"))
Note: For users, the testthat package is not required. However, if you would like to contribute to the debugging, testing, or improvement of ADMETShiny, please feel free to send your suggestions through of the e-mail or Feedback page.

For the “rCDK” & Webchem package is necessary the follow dependence:

Please, consider this option for the all features.
Java SE Development Kit Downloads by Oracle

Installation to the package in your environment with the follow steps:

You could consider install “pak”, follow the steps:
#Write in the R & RStudio console:
install.packages("pak")
#Call the package
library(pak)
#Follow this step: 
pak::pkg_install("xavierclementegarcia/ADMETShiny", subdir = "package")
You could install the package with “remotes”, :
#Write in the R & RStudio console:
install.packages("remotes")
#Call the package
library(remotes)
#Follow this step: 
remotes::install_github("xavierclementegarcia/ADMETShiny", subdir = "package")
Remember that the correct proficiency of the Shiny app is required JDK & the others dependencies listing above.

Launch ADMETShiny.

#Call to the package
library("admetshiny")
#For run the Shiny app
admetshiny::run_app()

title: Introduction to admetshiny
text: admetshiny is an R package that provides an interactive Shiny application and a toolbox of functions for the management, calculation, filtering, visualization and exploratory analysis of molecular descriptors and ADMET properties of small molecules. It integrates data exported from SwissADME, ADMETlab 3.0 and Deep-PK, and can compute descriptors locally via the Chemistry Development Kit (CDK).

title: Introduction to admetshiny
text: admetshiny is an R package that provides an interactive Shiny application and a toolbox of functions for the management, calculation, filtering, visualization and exploratory analysis of molecular descriptors and ADMET properties of small molecules. It integrates data exported from SwissADME, ADMETlab 3.0 and Deep-PK, and can compute descriptors locally via the Chemistry Development Kit (CDK).

title: Introduction to admetshiny
text:
Launching the application
The easiest way to use admetshiny is through its interactive application:
admetshiny::run_app()
The application provides four modules:

SwissADME Manager - upload a SwissADME CSV and apply drug-likeness filters.
CDK & webchem - retrieve SMILES from PubChem and calculate descriptors locally.
ADMETlab 3.0 - upload an ADMETlab CSV; properties are mapped automatically.
Deep-PK - upload a Deep-PK CSV; missing descriptors are calculated on-the-fly from SMILES.

title: Introduction to admetshiny
text:
Using the functions programmatically
The exported functions can also be used in plain R scripts.

Drug-likeness filters
library(admetshiny)

# Build a small toy dataset in the SwissADME schema
d <- data.frame(
  MW = c(300, 650),
  LogP = c(2, 7),
  TPSA = c(40, 160),
  MR = c(70, 150),
  "#H-bond acceptors" = c(4, 12),
  "#H-bond donors" = c(2, 7),
  "#Rotatable bonds" = c(3, 14),
  "#Heavy atoms" = c(20, 80),
  "#Aromatic heavy atoms" = c(6, 9),
  check.names = FALSE
)

# Add the violation columns required by the filters
d <- computeViolationColumns(d)

# Apply Lipinski + Veber
filtered <- applyFilters(d, filters = c("Lipinski", "Veber"))


Normalizing a SwissADME export
d <- read.csv("swissadme.csv", check.names = FALSE)
d <- fixSwissADME(d)


BOILED-Egg plot
# Requires LogP and TPSA columns
plotBoiledEgg(d)

title: Introduction to admetshiny
text:
Optional dependencies
Some features rely on suggested packages that are not installed automatically:



Feature
Package




CDK descriptors / Deep-PK processing
rcdk (requires Java JDK)


SMILES from PubChem
webchem


Radar plot
fmsb


t-SNE
Rtsne, ggrepel


PCA labels
ggrepel


Tanimoto / AGNES
rcdk, fingerprint, cluster


Parallel coordinates
GGally


Excel export
openxlsx



Install them with:
install.packages(c("rcdk", "webchem", "fmsb", "Rtsne", "ggrepel",
                   "fingerprint", "cluster", "GGally", "openxlsx"))
